rules.description: Minimum password length of at least 15 characters (NIST recommends 15+)
rules.recommendation: Set minimum length to 15+ characters. Length is the single most impactful password security control.
rules.breach_stat: 81% of hacking-related breaches involve weak or stolen passwords (Verizon DBIR).

rules.description: Maximum password length of at least 64 characters (NIST mandates no low maximum)
rules.recommendation: Set max length to at least 64 characters. Truncating passwords forces users toward weaker options.

rules.description: No arbitrary password expiry (NIST SP 800-63B §5.1.1.2 discourages forced rotation)
rules.recommendation: Remove periodic expiry. Forced rotation leads to predictable patterns like Password1 → Password2.
rules.breach_stat: Forced password changes increase reuse by up to 41% (UNC Chapel Hill study).

rules.description: No mandatory complexity rules (NIST discourages arbitrary character-class requirements)
rules.recommendation: Drop character-class mandates. They cause users to create predictable substitutions like P@ssw0rd!
rules.breach_stat: Complexity rules are among the top causes of weak-but-technically-compliant passwords.

rules.description: Passwords checked against known breach databases (NIST §5.1.1.2)
rules.recommendation: Integrate HaveIBeenPwned or a similar service to block known-compromised passwords at authentication.
rules.breach_stat: Over 550 million compromised passwords are publicly searchable. Your users reuse them.

rules.description: Multi-factor authentication enforced alongside passwords (NIST AAL2)
rules.recommendation: Mandate MFA. A breached password is game over without a second factor.
rules.breach_stat: MFA blocks 99.9% of automated account compromise attacks (Microsoft, 2019).
